title: Truck Accidents
description: Accidents involving commercial trucks, semi-trucks, and 18-wheelers are among the most serious on the road. Federal regulations, multiple liable parties, and severe injuries make these cases significantly more complex than standard car accidents.
metaTitle: Truck Accident Guide — What To Do After a Semi-Truck Crash
metaDescription: What to do after a truck accident in California or Arizona. Steps, evidence checklist, federal regulation issues, and when to speak with an attorney.

commonCauses.title: Driver Fatigue
commonCauses.description: Federal Hours of Service regulations limit driving time, but violations are common. Drowsy truck drivers have significantly impaired reaction times.

commonCauses.title: Distracted Driving
commonCauses.description: Cell phone use, dispatching devices, and other distractions are particularly dangerous at commercial vehicle speeds and weights.

commonCauses.title: Improper Loading
commonCauses.description: Overloaded or improperly secured cargo can cause trucks to tip, jackknife, or shed debris onto the roadway.

commonCauses.title: Brake Failure
commonCauses.description: Commercial trucks require significantly more stopping distance. Brake maintenance failures are a leading mechanical cause of accidents.

commonCauses.title: Speeding and Aggressive Driving
commonCauses.description: Delivery and schedule pressure can push drivers to speed or drive aggressively, especially on highways.

commonCauses.title: Inadequate Training
commonCauses.description: Improperly trained drivers may be unprepared for adverse weather, emergency maneuvers, or hazardous cargo handling.

commonCauses.title: DUI / Impairment
commonCauses.description: Commercial drivers are held to a lower legal BAC limit (0.04%) than non-commercial drivers, but violations still occur.

immediateSteps.title: Call 911 immediately
immediateSteps.description: Truck accidents frequently involve serious injuries. Call 911 even if you believe injuries are minor — symptoms can be delayed.

immediateSteps.title: Do not move injured parties
immediateSteps.description: Spinal injuries are common in truck accidents. Avoid moving anyone unless they are in immediate danger.

immediateSteps.title: Photograph everything before vehicles are moved
immediateSteps.description: Trucks are often moved quickly for traffic management. Document skid marks, final vehicle positions, road conditions, and all damage before anything is cleared.

immediateSteps.title: Get the truck's DOT number and carrier information
immediateSteps.description: Located on the truck's door or cab. This identifies the carrier and is essential for investigating insurance and regulatory compliance.

immediateSteps.title: Request the driver's log book information
immediateSteps.description: Federal law requires commercial drivers to keep Hours of Service logs. This is critical evidence that can disappear quickly.

immediateSteps.title: Identify any witnesses
immediateSteps.description: Other drivers, passengers, or bystanders who saw the accident. Independent witness accounts are especially important when dealing with large carriers.

immediateSteps.title: Seek immediate medical evaluation
immediateSteps.description: Truck accident injuries are often severe. Even if you feel functional, internal injuries and traumatic brain injuries may not be immediately apparent.

immediateSteps.title: Preserve all records
immediateSteps.description: Keep everything — medical bills, repair estimates, rental records, and documentation of any lost work.

items: Photos of all vehicles (all angles, before anything moves)
Truck's DOT number, license plate, and company name
Driver's name, CDL number, and contact information
Photos of skid marks, road conditions, and debris
Names and contact info of all witnesses
Driver's Hours of Service logbook (request via attorney)
Truck's black box / event data recorder
All specialist and follow-up visit notes
Vehicle repair or total loss documentation
Weigh station records for the vehicle
Photos of the accident scene taken after the fact

timelineRisks.risk: Critical truck data can be overwritten
timelineRisks.action: Electronic logging device data and black box recordings may be overwritten within days. An attorney can send a spoliation letter to preserve this evidence.

timelineRisks.risk: Trucking company begins its own investigation
timelineRisks.action: Carriers dispatch investigators and attorneys quickly after serious accidents. Documenting your account and evidence early is essential.
Report the accident to your insurer promptly. Avoid giving recorded statements to the trucking company's insurer without legal guidance.
Collect and preserve witness statements. Obtain the police report once available.

timelineRisks.risk: Statute of limitations for personal injury
timelineRisks.action: California generally allows 2 years from the accident date to file a personal injury lawsuit. Claims against government entities may have shorter deadlines.

timelineRisks.risk: Statute of limitations for personal injury
timelineRisks.action: Arizona also has a 2-year statute of limitations for personal injury claims. Consult an attorney early to protect your options.

insuranceIssues.explanation: Truck accidents may involve the driver, the trucking company, the cargo loader, a maintenance contractor, or the truck manufacturer. Identifying all responsible parties is complex and consequential.
Commercial trucks are required to carry significantly higher liability insurance than passenger vehicles — often $750,000 to $5 million. This also means carriers have experienced claims teams.

Large trucking companies often have dedicated legal and claims teams. They may contact you quickly after an accident with settlement offers. Early offers are typically below the full value of damages.
Some truck drivers operate as independent contractors rather than employees, which can affect which insurance policies apply and who bears liability.
FMCSA violations (hours of service, drug testing, maintenance requirements) can establish negligence. These records must be requested before they are destroyed.

whenToGetLawyer: You or any passenger sustained injuries requiring medical treatment
The trucking company or its insurer contacted you directly after the accident
You received a settlement offer before fully understanding your injuries
The truck driver was fatigued, impaired, or in violation of federal regulations
There are multiple potentially liable parties (driver, carrier, cargo loader)
Your injuries have caused significant lost wages or long-term disability
The truck was operated by a government entity
You are unsure how to preserve electronic evidence before it is overwritten
